topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I stared at the printed memo, the words blurring into meaningless ink. Sarah had the metrics, the undeniable track record, and now, inexplicably, she was getting the corner office. I clenched my jaw so tightly my molars ached. Every time I saw the congratulations directed toward her, a hot, tight sensation coiled low in my stomach. "Of course," I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible over the keyboard clicking furiously. I shoved my chair back, the scrape echoing too loudly in the quiet cubicle farm. I needed to do something. I needed to prove that the hours I poured into that Q3 project were more valuable than whatever vague, subjective criteria the management was using. My hands shook slightly as I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic feeling suddenly heavy and cold in my grasp.

[story 2]

I watched from across the breakroom as Mark accepted the commendation. His smile was practiced, smooth, and utterly devoid of the genuine warmth I’d seen directed toward me in the last six months. A deep, burning pressure built behind my eyes, making the edges of the room seem sharp and distorted. I leaned against the counter, trying to keep my breathing even, but it felt shallow, ragged. "That's just the way the wind blows, isn't it?" I asked, my voice pitched a little too high, a brittle edge creeping into the tone. Mark turned, his expression shifting into one of weary patience. He didn't meet my gaze directly; he looked past me, toward the window. The sheer unfairness of it all made my fists tighten at my sides, knuckles whitening against the laminate countertop. I wanted to slam my mug down, to make a noise that would shatter the polite veneer of the morning.

[story 3]

The air in the conference room was thick, suffocatingly formal. When the CEO finally announced the decision, a wave of heat washed over me, making the fine hairs on my arms prickle. I sat perfectly still, focusing intensely on the grain of the mahogany table, refusing to let my gaze drift to the two figures seated at the head of the table. I could feel the blood pounding a frantic rhythm in my temples. When the announcement concluded, I stood up abruptly, the chair protesting loudly beneath me. "I see," I managed, the words coming out clipped and unnaturally flat. I walked toward the door, my steps deliberate and heavy, each movement radiating a contained tension that vibrated through the room. I didn't look back, but I could feel the weight of expectation pressing down on my shoulders, a crushing, immovable force.
